questions.scenario: A product review lists four promises for `devpolaris-orders-api`: keep checkout records consistent, let customers download receipts, remember payment retries for seven days, and support a temporary legacy folder. Which service matches are reasonable?

options.text: Azure SQL Database for checkout records that need relational consistency.
Blob Storage for receipt files and daily export objects.
Cosmos DB for short-lived known-key retry records.
A single managed disk for every promise because one disk is easier to back up.

questions.explanation: Different promises call for different services. A managed disk is useful for VM-shaped storage, but it is not the right general answer for relational records, object downloads, and known-key TTL data.

questions.scenario: A team picks Cosmos DB because it sounds flexible, then adds SQL-like reporting requirements a month later. What decision habit would have reduced this risk?

options.text: Write a short decision record that names the access pattern, consistency need, retention, and restore expectation before choosing the service.
Choose the newest managed service because newer services cover older database use cases.
Avoid documenting tradeoffs until after the feature has real traffic.
Use the service with the smallest resource name to keep IaC readable.

questions.explanation: A small decision record forces the team to describe behavior before choosing a service. It makes later changes easier to review because the original assumption is visible.
